2,Keep Interfaces Small (1-3 methods),Design,"interfaces, single responsibility, small","Prefer small interfaces with one to three methods. The Go standard library exemplifies this with io.Reader and io.Writer.",Define focused interfaces with only the methods needed by the consumer. 3,Define Interfaces at Consumer Side,Design,"interfaces, consumer, dependency inversion","Interfaces should be defined in the package that uses them, not in the package that implements them. This follows the dependency inversion principle and keeps packages decoupled.",Define the interface where it is consumed. Let the implementing package remain unaware of the interface.,Define interfaces in the provider package and force consumers to import them.,"package orderservice
// Bad: interface defined in the provider package

6,Empty Interface (any) - when acceptable,Design,"any, empty interface, type safety","The empty interface (any or interface{}) should be used sparingly. It is acceptable for truly generic operations like encoding/json.Marshal or fmt.Println, but not as a substitute for proper typing.",Use any only when the function truly must handle all types such as serialization or logging. Use generics when possible for type safety.,Use any to avoid designing proper interfaces or types. Do not use any as a lazy parameter type.,"package logger
// Bad: using any instead of proper types
func Add(a any, b any) any {

7,Type Assertions vs Type Switches,Design,"type assertion, type switch, runtime typing","Use type switches for handling multiple types cleanly. Use single type assertions with the comma-ok idiom to safely check one type. Never use bare type assertions without checking.",Use the comma-ok idiom for single type assertions. Use type switches when checking multiple types. Always handle the default case.,Use bare type assertions without checking which will panic on wrong type. Avoid deeply nested type assertion chains.,"package handler
// Bad: bare assertion panics if wrong type
// Bad: nested type assertions are fragile

column1: 8,Implicit Interface Satisfaction,Design,"implicit, duck typing, structural typing","Go interfaces are satisfied implicitly. A type does not declare which interfaces it implements. This allows defining interfaces after the fact and keeps packages decoupled.",Let types satisfy interfaces implicitly. Do not add unnecessary comments listing which interfaces a type implements.,Try to mimic explicit interface declarations from other languages. Do not create registration mechanisms for interface satisfaction.,"package audio

9,Stringer Interface,Standard Library,"Stringer, fmt, string representation","Implement the fmt.Stringer interface (String() string) to provide a human-readable representation of your types. This is used by fmt.Print, fmt.Sprintf with %v and %s verbs.",Implement String() string on types that benefit from a readable representation. Keep the output concise and informative.,Omit Stringer on types that are frequently printed. Do not include sensitive data in String() output.,"package user
// Bad: no Stringer, prints as raw integer

10,error Interface,Standard Library,"error, interface, custom errors","The error interface is Go's standard mechanism for error handling. Custom error types should implement the error interface and can carry additional context for programmatic error inspection.",Create custom error types that implement the error interface. Use errors.Is and errors.As for inspection. Wrap errors with %w.,Use sentinel string comparisons for error checking. Do not create error types without useful context or methods.,"package apperr

11,sort.Interface Pattern,Standard Library,"sort, Interface, Len, Less, Swap","Implement sort.Interface (Len, Less, Swap) for custom sorting of slices. For simpler cases, use sort.Slice with a closure. The interface pattern is useful for complex or reusable sort orders.",Implement sort.Interface for reusable or complex sorting. Use sort.Slice for one-off simple sorts. Keep Less consistent and transitive.,Re-implement sorting algorithms manually. Do not use unstable comparisons in Less that could cause non-deterministic results.,"package employee
func (b BySalary) Len() int           { return len(b) }
func (b BySalary) Less(i, j int) bool { return b[i].Salary < b[j].Salary }
func (b BySalary) Swap(i, j int)      { b[i], b[j] = b[j], b[i] }
// Bad: manual bubble sort instead of sort.Interface

12,http.Handler / http.HandlerFunc,Standard Library,"http, Handler, HandlerFunc, middleware","Use http.Handler and http.HandlerFunc to build composable HTTP middleware and handlers. http.HandlerFunc adapts a function to the http.Handler interface enabling functional handler composition.",Use http.Handler for composable middleware chains. Use http.HandlerFunc to adapt plain functions. Chain middleware by wrapping handlers.,Put all logic in a single large handler function. Do not skip the http.Handler interface for middleware patterns.,"package server
// Bad: monolithic handler with no middleware composition

This gives you full control over %v, %s, %q, and custom verbs for your types.",Implement fmt.Formatter when you need different representations for different format verbs. 14,Interface for Testing (DI),Testing,"testing, dependency injection, mock, interface","Define interfaces at the test boundary to enable dependency injection and mocking. This allows replacing real dependencies with test doubles without modifying production code.",Define small interfaces for external dependencies. Inject dependencies via constructor functions. Create simple test doubles that implement the interface.,Depend directly on concrete external clients in your business logic making it impossible to test without real services.,"package order

15,Don't Export Interfaces for Mocking,Testing,"export, mock, interface, package boundary","Do not export interfaces solely for the purpose of allowing consumers to mock your package. Let consumers define their own interfaces with only the methods they need.",Keep interfaces unexported when they are only used internally. Let consumers define narrow interfaces that match their needs.,Export large interfaces from your library package just so users can mock them in tests.,"package database
// Unexported: consumers define their own interface
// Bad: exported interface solely for mocking

16,Interface Pollution (too many interfaces),Anti-Pattern,"pollution, over-abstraction, premature","Interface pollution occurs when interfaces are created prematurely or unnecessarily. Interfaces should emerge from a real need for abstraction, not be created upfront for every type.",Create interfaces when you have a concrete need for polymorphism or decoupling. Wait until you have at least two implementations or a testing need.,Create an interface for every struct. Do not define interfaces before you have a concrete use case requiring abstraction.,"package cache
// No interface needed: only one implementation, used directly
// Bad: premature interface with only one implementation

17,encoding.BinaryMarshaler/TextMarshaler,Standard Library,"encoding, marshal, BinaryMarshaler, TextMarshaler","Implement encoding.BinaryMarshaler/BinaryUnmarshaler and encoding.TextMarshaler/TextUnmarshaler to control how your types are serialized. TextMarshaler is used by encoding/json for map keys.",Implement TextMarshaler and TextUnmarshaler for types used as JSON map keys or in text-based formats. Implement BinaryMarshaler for compact binary serialization.,Rely on default serialization when your type needs a specific format. Do not ignore the Unmarshaler counterpart when implementing a Marshaler.,"package geo
// Bad: using non-standard delimiter, no round-trip support

18,Functional Interfaces (single-method to function type),Design,"functional, adapter, single-method, function type","When an interface has a single method, consider using a function type instead. This is the pattern used by http.HandlerFunc. It allows passing simple functions without creating a struct.",Use function types for single-method interfaces when stateless behavior suffices. Provide an adapter type so functions can satisfy the interface.,Force users to create structs for trivial single-method interface implementations. Do not over-complicate simple callback patterns.,"package events
// HandlerFunc is an adapter allowing use of ordinary functions as Handlers
// Bad: forces users to create a struct for every handler

19,Interface Guards (var _ Interface = (*Struct)(nil)),Design,"compile-time check, interface guard, assertion","Use compile-time interface guards to verify that a type satisfies an interface at compile time. This catches missing methods early and documents the intended interface compliance.",Add compile-time checks using var _ Interface = (*Struct)(nil) to verify interface satisfaction. Place them near the type definition.,Skip compile-time checks and discover missing method implementations only at runtime when a type assertion or function call fails.,"package queue
// Compile-time check: InMemoryQueue must satisfy MessageQueue
